comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a practical adaptation of topic-model-style user clustering for large-scale online advertising. The authors modify the pLSA independence assumptions so that cluster assignment for a user can be inferred at run-time from the user’s beacon/event history (p(cluster|user)=sum_b p(cluster|b)p(b|user)). They describe a scalable Hadoop/Pig implementation, report convergence and runtime on a large dataset (≈13k beacons, ≈500M users), and provide interpretability examples and an A/B online experiment showing improved eCPC/eCPA vs. an in-house cosine-similarity clustering baseline.

Strengths:
- Addresses a real industrial challenge: volatile large user populations and the need to assign clusters at ad-serving time without storing per-user cluster parameters.
- The modeling tweak (making cluster independent of user given beacon) is simple, well-motivated for the domain, and enables efficient run-time assignment and reduced storage.
- Presents an end-to-end system: algorithmic derivation, EM training, distributed implementation details, convergence/runtime numbers, and live A/B evaluation—valuable to practitioners.
- Qualitative cluster examples suggest semantic coherence, and the live experiment demonstrates practical uplift in core business metrics.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Comparisons are limited. The only quantitative baseline is a proprietary cosine-similarity/K-means approach. There is no comparison to better-established scalable methods (e.g., distributed LDA implementations, matrix factorization, or simpler supervised baselines), nor to offline predictive metrics (AUC, log-loss) which would help generalize results beyond one production baseline.
- Experimental reporting lacks detail and statistical rigor. Plots are anonymized and no absolute numbers, variances, or significance tests are provided for the A/B results; this makes it hard to judge the magnitude and reliability of the reported gains.
- The choice of many modelling/hyperparameters is under-explained: why 600 clusters, why 60 days of history, hard vs soft clustering choice, initialization strategy, regularization or smoothing for rare beacons, and how the beacon set was sampled (removal of tails then sampling). These choices might materially affect results.
- Handling of users with very sparse or zero beacon histories is not discussed (cold-start), nor are temporal dynamics of beacons (ageing of events) or how often models are retrained in production.
- Privacy/selection bias and robustness: the beacon selection and sampling may bias clusters toward more active users; implications for advertisers and for representativeness are not discussed.

Soundness:
The probabilistic derivation and the modified EM are reasonable and appear correct for the stated independence assumption. The implementation and parallelization strategy are sensible. However, some practicalities (smoothing, numerical stability, treatment of zero probabilities) are not detailed.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add quantitative offline metrics (AUC, log-loss) on held-out data in addition to the online A/B to allow reproducibility and easier benchmarking.
- Include at least one additional baseline (e.g., distributed LDA, simple supervised models, or matrix factorization) or ablation showing the contribution of key design choices (cluster count, history window, beacon selection strategy).
- Report absolute performance numbers, confidence intervals, and statistical tests for the A/B experiment to assess significance and effect size.
- Discuss cold-start users, temporal decay for beacons, and operational model refresh cadence.
- Provide more detail on beacon pre-processing, sampling, and any regularization applied during EM to help others reproduce and understand robustness to rare beacons.

Recommendation summary:
This is a solid applied paper with clear practical value for industrial advertising systems. It offers an effective engineering solution to a real deployment challenge and demonstrates live-system gains. For an academic venue, the paper would be strengthened by broader baselines, clearer experimental statistics, and more discussion of design choices and limitations. I recommend acceptance as an applied contribution (score 7/10).
